section.title: May the Lord Answer in Trouble
section.sectionRationale: A royal psalm of communal intercession, likely sung by the people over their king before he went out to battle, pairing naturally with Psalm 21's thanksgiving after victory. It contrasts trust in chariots and horses with trust in the name of the LORD, a contrast that runs throughout Israel's history and anticipates the greater King whose victory did not come by military might.
section.thesis: The people's prayer for their king's success rests not on the strength of chariots or horses but entirely on the name of the LORD, a pattern of trust that finds its fullest expression in the victory of the greater King, Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the congregation's prayer that the LORD would answer and support the king; their confident wish that his petitions be granted, followed by present tense confidence that the LORD saves His anointed; and the closing contrast between trust in chariots and horses versus trust in the name of the LORD.

units.label: May the Lord answer you
units.note: the people's prayer for the king

verses.text: May the LORD answer you in the day of trouble; may the name of the God of Jacob protect you.
May He send you help from the sanctuary and sustain you from Zion.
May He remember all your gifts and look favorably on your burnt offerings. {{selah|Selah}}
May He give you the desires of your heart and make all your plans succeed.

units.label: Now I know the Lord saves His anointed

verses.text:
May we shout for joy at your victory and raise a banner in the name of our God. 
May the LORD grant all your petitions.

Now I know that the LORD saves His {{mashiach|anointed}}; He answers him from His holy heaven 
with the saving power of His right hand.

units.label: We trust in the name of the Lord
units.note: chariots and horses versus the name of the Lord

verses.text: Some trust in chariots and others in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD our God.
They collapse and fall, but we rise up and stand firm.
O LORD, save the king. Answer us on the day we call.

selah.gloss: A musical or liturgical notation of uncertain precise meaning, likely indicating a pause, a rest, or a moment for reflection or instrumental interlude within the singing of the psalm.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, Messiah, one set apart for a God given office by anointing oil. Here applied to Israel's king, whose victories the people trace directly to God's saving power, a title and pattern of dependence later fulfilled completely in Jesus, the Messiah.

basic.context: This psalm captures the people of Israel praying together over their king before he goes out to battle, asking that the LORD would answer him, remember his offerings, and grant the desires of his heart. In the middle of the prayer, confidence rises, 'Now I know that the LORD saves His anointed'. The psalm closes with its most memorable line: some trust in chariots and horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD our God, a trust that alone leads to standing firm rather than collapsing.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm reflects the specific ancient practice of communal intercession for the king before battle, but its underlying principle, trusting God's name rather than human military strength, applies to every believer facing any kind of conflict or trial, not only kings facing armies.
basic.originalLanguages: The congregation prays for the king using selah, a pause for reflection, marking key moments in the prayer, and confidently names him God's mashiach, anointed one, whose victories come not from his own resources but from the saving power of God's right hand.
basic.typology: Israel's anointed king, dependent entirely on God rather than chariots or horses for victory, foreshadows Christ, the true and final Anointed One, whose decisive victory over sin and death came not through worldly power but through the seeming weakness of the cross.
basic.christ: The people's confidence that 'the LORD saves His anointed' anticipates, in its fullest sense, God's saving of Jesus Christ, His anointed Son, whose victory over death through resurrection came not by chariots or horses, worldly power, but by the greater power of God displayed through apparent weakness, 1 Corinthians 1:25.
basic.god: God is shown responding to communal, intercessory prayer for His anointed leader, and as the true source of any real victory, contrasted sharply with human military resources like chariots and horses that ultimately fail.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 21 is the natural companion thanksgiving psalm after this prayer's battle. Proverbs 21:31 states the horse is made ready for battle, but victory belongs to the LORD. Zechariah 4:6 declares victory comes not by might or power but by God's Spirit.
basic.oneStory: This communal prayer for Israel's king to be saved not by chariots or horses but by the name of the LORD anticipates the far greater pattern fulfilled in Christ, whose victory over sin and death came through the apparent weakness of the cross rather than worldly might, proving once again that salvation belongs to the LORD.

context.literary: A royal psalm structured as communal intercession, moving from prayer for the king's success in battle, through rising confidence expressed in first person plural and then first person singular, to a closing contrast between military trust and trust in God's name, likely sung antiphonally by priests, king, and people.
context.historical: Reflects the ancient Israelite practice of seeking God's favor through sacrifice and prayer before a king went to war, set against the wider ancient Near Eastern context where chariots and cavalry represented the height of military technology and power.
context.canonical: Forms a matched pair with Psalm 21, prayer before battle followed by thanksgiving after victory, and its central contrast between trusting military strength and trusting God's name echoes throughout the historical books, prophets, and wisdom literature.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lead Israel's corporate worship in interceding for their king before battle, and to instill in the whole congregation the conviction that ultimate security and victory come from trusting the LORD's name, not human military might.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific liturgical practice of praying for Israel's king before battle; its prescriptive, enduring principle is the call to trust God's name rather than worldly resources or power in every kind of conflict or trial.

debates.question: Who is the speaker in verse 6, 'Now I know that the LORD saves His anointed', shifting from the plural 'we' of the earlier verses?

views.label: A priest or worship leader speaking a word of assurance to the king and congregation, perhaps after receiving a favorable sign or oracle
views.case: The shift from communal plea, verses 1-5, to confident personal declaration, verse 6, and back to plural in verses 7-9, suggests a liturgical structure where a priest or prophetic voice answers the congregation's prayer with assurance mid psalm.

views.label: The king himself speaking in the first person, expressing his own confidence in response to the people's intercession on his behalf
views.case: As the psalm's central figure and the one whose anointing, mashiach, is directly named, it is fitting for the king to voice his own settled trust at the psalm's turning point, responding to the people who have just prayed for him.

debates.bearing: Either reading preserves the psalm's essential structure and message, communal prayer met with confident assurance that God saves His anointed; the identity of the speaker in verse 6 does not change the psalm's central theological point about trusting God rather than human strength.

study.originalLanguages: The congregation's prayer includes selah, a marked pause for reflection, and centers on Israel's king as God's mashiach, anointed one, whose victory the people confidently trace not to chariots or horses but to the saving power of God's own right hand.

typology.type: Israel's anointed king, saved by God rather than by chariots or horses, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, the true Anointed One, whose victory over sin and death came through the apparent weakness of the cross rather than worldly military power.

typology.type: The contrast between trusting chariots and horses versus trusting the name of the LORD, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament's consistent teaching that God's power is made perfect in weakness, and that spiritual victory does not come through worldly strength, 2 Corinthians 12:9-10.

study.christ: The congregation's confidence that 'the LORD saves His anointed' finds its fullest and truest expression in God's saving of Jesus Christ, whose victory over sin and death was accomplished not through chariots, horses, or any worldly display of power, but through the crucifixion and resurrection, the very place where God's power was most fully displayed through apparent weakness, exactly the pattern this psalm's closing contrast anticipates.
study.god: God is shown responding to the corporate, intercessory prayer of His people on behalf of their anointed leader, and is revealed as the true and only source of victory, in contrast to human military strength which ultimately collapses and fails.

crossReferences.note: The natural companion psalm, moving from this prayer before battle to thanksgiving for the king's victory afterward.
States plainly that the horse is prepared for the day of battle, but victory belongs to the LORD.
Declares that victory comes not by might nor by power, but by God's Spirit, echoing this psalm's central contrast.

study.oneStory: This communal prayer for Israel's king, trusting not in chariots or horses but in the name of the LORD, anticipates the far greater pattern fulfilled in Jesus Christ, whose ultimate victory over sin and death came not through worldly power but through the cross, proving once more that salvation belongs to the LORD alone.

quiz.q: What is the setting and purpose of this psalm?

options.text: A communal prayer of the people interceding for their king before he goes out to battle.
options.note: The psalm's language of trouble, sanctuary, offerings, and victory points to this specific pre-battle intercession.

options.text: A private prayer of thanksgiving after a personal healing.
options.note: The psalm is corporate in voice, 'we' and 'you', and concerns the king's battle, not a private healing.

options.text: A funeral lament for a fallen king.
options.note: The psalm looks forward to victory and answered prayer, not backward to mourning a death.

options.text: A wisdom instruction with no reference to the king.
options.note: The king is directly and repeatedly addressed throughout, making this a royal psalm, not general wisdom instruction.

quiz.q: What confident statement appears in verse 6?

options.text: 'Now I know that the LORD saves His anointed.'
options.note: This is the psalm's central turning point of rising confidence within the prayer.

options.text: 'The LORD has abandoned His anointed.'
options.note: This is the opposite of the psalm's actual declaration of confidence in God's salvation.

options.text: 'The king trusts only in his chariots.'
options.note: The psalm explicitly contrasts trust in chariots with trust in God's name, and the king's trust is placed in the LORD, not chariots.

options.text: 'No one can know whether the LORD will act.'
options.note: This directly contradicts the confident, settled tone of verse 6.

quiz.q: What contrast does the psalm draw in verse 7?

options.text: Some trust in chariots and others in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD our God.
options.note: This is the psalm's most memorable and theologically central line.

options.text: Some trust in wealth, but we trust in wisdom.
options.note: The psalm's specific contrast is military resources, chariots and horses, versus trust in God's name, not wealth versus wisdom.

options.text: Some trust in prophets, but we trust in priests.
options.note: The contrast is between military might and trust in God, not between different human offices.

options.text: Some trust in the past, but we trust in the future.
options.note: This misses the psalm's specific and concrete contrast between military strength and trust in God's name.

quiz.q: What is the result of trusting chariots and horses versus trusting the LORD's name, according to verse 8?

options.text: Those who trust chariots and horses collapse and fall, but those who trust the LORD rise up and stand firm.
options.note: This vivid contrast shows the ultimate futility of human military strength apart from God.

options.text: Both groups experience identical outcomes regardless of where they place their trust.
options.note: The psalm draws a sharp distinction in outcomes, not an identical result for both groups.

options.text: Those who trust chariots gain lasting victory, while those who trust the LORD eventually fail.
options.note: This reverses the psalm's actual teaching, which favors trust in the LORD as leading to standing firm.

options.text: The psalm offers no comment on the outcome of either kind of trust.
options.note: Verse 8 explicitly states the outcome, collapse for one, standing firm for the other.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's pattern anticipate Christ?

options.text: It anticipates God saving His Anointed not through worldly military power but through what appeared to be weakness, as Christ's victory came through the cross.
options.note: The contrast between worldly strength and trust in God's name mirrors the pattern of God's power displayed through the apparent weakness of the crucifixion.

options.text: It predicts a specific future battle Jesus would fight with literal chariots.
options.note: The psalm's contrast is thematic and typological, not a literal prediction of Jesus using chariots in battle.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament's teaching about Christ's victory.
options.note: The pattern of victory through apparent weakness rather than worldly strength connects clearly to New Testament teaching, such as 2 Corinthians 12:9-10.

options.text: It teaches that military strength is always sufficient for victory.
options.note: This is precisely the opposite of the psalm's teaching, which favors trust in God over military strength.

questions: This psalm shows a whole community praying together for their leader before a difficult challenge. Who in your life, a leader, a family member, a friend, could use this kind of intentional, corporate prayer support from you this week?

The people's confidence rises mid-prayer: 'Now I know that the LORD saves His anointed.' Have you experienced a moment where your own confidence in God grew stronger even while still in the middle of praying about something unresolved?

The psalm contrasts trusting chariots and horses with trusting the name of the LORD. What is your equivalent of 'chariots and horses', the resource, strategy, or strength you are tempted to trust in instead of God?

Verse 8 says those who trust human strength collapse, while those who trust the LORD stand firm. Where in your life right now do you need to consciously shift your trust from your own resources to God's name?
Christ's decisive victory came through the apparent weakness of the cross, not worldly power. How does this reshape the way you think about strength and weakness in your own circumstances?

translationNotes.note: Selah likely marks a pause for musical interlude or reflection; its precise musical function remains uncertain, though it consistently highlights significant moments in a psalm.
Anointed renders mashiach, applied here to Israel's king, the same title, Messiah, later given in its fullest sense to Jesus Christ.

Chariots and horses represent the height of ancient military technology; the psalm's contrast with trusting the LORD's name would have been immediately understood as a comparison between worldly power and covenant faith.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 20:1-9. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file.
